conversation.speaker: user
conversation.text: I used to be a massive proponent of microservices. I genuinely believed breaking everything into isolated, independent services was the only way to avoid a spaghetti codebase.

conversation.speaker: assistant
conversation.text: Microservices definitely help isolate domain logic and scale teams independently.

conversation.speaker: user
conversation.text: Until you hit the distributed monolith problem. The network latency and deployment coordination became a nightmare. I completely abandoned that view and became convinced that a modular monolith is the single best architectural pattern for 95% of businesses.

conversation.speaker: assistant
conversation.text: Modular monoliths have seen a huge resurgence lately for exactly those reasons.

conversation.speaker: user
conversation.text: But you know what? As our infrastructure team scaled, the merge conflicts and deployment bottlenecks in our single codebase returned. I've come to believe that neither extreme works; a service-per-team architecture, where bounded contexts align exactly with organizational reporting lines, is the correct mental model.

query: What is the user's latest viewpoint on microservices versus monolithic architectures?

must_not_contain: modular monolith is the single best
